title: Motivic Cohomology of Singular Schemes: a dégustation
speaker: Elden Elmanto

proof_stages.label: Affine cone and blow-up square
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a smooth projective variety X embedded by O(1), defines its affine cone C(X), and records that blowing up the vertex gives the total space of the tautological line bundle over X with exceptional divisor X, producing the geometric square used throughout.

proof_stages.label: Zero-cycle subgroup and vanishing theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines K0 via perfect complexes, introduces the subgroup f_dK0 generated by smooth closed points as an avatar of zero-cycles, states the desired vanishing theorem for f_{dim X+1}K0(C(X)), and reviews earlier cases and hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Motivic cohomology machinery for singular schemes
proof_stages.summary: A filtration on K-theory extending classical motivic cohomology to singular schemes is introduced, together with the required properties: singular Nesterenko–Suslin comparison, motivic Soulé vanishing, A1-invariantization to cdh motivic cohomology, blow-up descent, and syntomic/de Rham comparison squares.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from f_dK0 to top motivic cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Using the edge map H^{2d,d}(Y) -> K0(Y), singular comparison with Milnor K-theory, and Kato–Saito input, the proof reduces the desired vanishing of f_dK0 for the cone to proving H^{2d}(C(X),Z(d))=0.

proof_stages.label: Cdh contraction of the cone and p-completion reduction
proof_stages.summary: Cdh descent applied to the blow-up square and A1-invariance identify the cdh motivic cohomology of the cone with that of the base field; the syntomic pullback square and vanishing ranges then show the target motivic group is derived p-complete, so it suffices to kill it after mod p reduction.

proof_stages.label: Syntomic reduction to the smooth blow-up
proof_stages.summary: The mod p obstruction is rewritten through cdh/syntomic descent as a statement about syntomic cohomology of the smooth blow-up of the cone, with a vanishing target in dimensions at least three and a separate degree-three surjectivity statement for cones over curves.

proof_stages.label: Log-form and coherent-cohomology vanishing
proof_stages.summary: The final syntomic groups are translated into cohomology of top logarithmic de Rham–Witt forms, then controlled by the fiber sequence involving top forms and Cartier and by an explicit pushforward computation along the line-bundle projection, reducing the result to elementary coherent cohomology vanishing.

examples.name: Ordinary topological contraction of a cone
examples.note: The speaker compares the affine cone with a topological cone that contracts to its vertex, illustrating—but not proving—the expectation that cone invariants should be small.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only invokes the topological idea that a cone contracts to a point as an analogy for why invariants might be small

passes.reason: A topological cone is contractible to a point, used only as a heuristic comparison for small invariants.
A topological cone is contractible, i.e. collapsed to the homotopy type of a point, and is used only as a heuristic comparison.
A topological cone is being viewed through its contraction to a point, a collapsed/contractible homotopy type, used only as intuition rather than to refute a claim.
a topological cone is contractible and is being collapsed to a point, used only as a contrast with motivic behavior.
a topological cone is contractible and collapses to the apex, used only as a heuristic contrast with motivic invariants.
A topological cone contracts/collapses to its apex, giving a trivial homotopy type; it is used as intuition, not to refute a claim.
The topological cone is contractible to a point, a collapsed homotopy type used only as a heuristic contrast rather than to refute a claim.
A topological cone is used as the contractible-to-a-point heuristic, not to refute a claim, and its homotopy type is collapsed/pointlike.
A topological cone is contractible and collapses to a point, used as intuition rather than as a refutation.
a topological cone is homotopically collapsed to its vertex and is used only as an analogy for small invariants
A topological cone has the collapsed contractible homotopy type of a point and is used only as an analogy for small invariants.
A topological cone is used as a contractible-to-a-point heuristic, not to refute a claim, and is homotopically collapsed.

examples.name: Bloch–Srinivas counterexamples to K0(C(X)) = Z
examples.note: The cited family with nonzero H^1(X,O_X(1)) shows the naive smallness statement for all of K0 is false and motivates shifting to the smaller zero-cycle-like subgroup f_dK0.
examples.engagement_reason: the Bloch–Srinivas examples are cited as showing K0 of the cone can be larger than Z, without constructing or analyzing them

passes.reason: Bloch–Srinivas examples are cited explicitly to refute the guess that K0(C(X)) equals Z.
The Bloch–Srinivas examples are explicitly invoked to show the expected equality K0(C(X)) = Z fails.
The Bloch–Srinivas cones with nonzero H^1 are explicitly invoked to show the guess K0(C(X))=Z is false, but they are not sporadic or extremal objects.
the Bloch–Srinivas examples are invoked explicitly to show that the guess K0(C(X)) = Z can fail.
Bloch–Srinivas examples are invoked explicitly to refute the expectation that K0(C(X)) equals Z.
Bloch–Srinivas examples are invoked precisely to show that the asserted equality K0(C(X)) = Z can fail.
The Bloch–Srinivas cones form a systematic obstruction showing that the proposed equality K0(C(X))=Z can fail.
The Bloch–Srinivas examples are explicitly invoked to disprove the guess that K0(C(X))=Z, but they form an ordinary family of cones rather than a sporadic or extremal object.
Bloch–Srinivas examples are explicitly invoked to disprove the expectation that K0(C(X)) equals Z.
the Bloch–Srinivas examples are invoked to refute the expectation that K0(C(X)) equals Z
The Bloch–Srinivas examples are explicitly invoked to refute the proposed equality K0(C(X))=Z.
Bloch–Srinivas examples are explicitly invoked to disprove the guess K0(C(X)) = Z, while forming a systematic family rather than a sporadic structural exception.

examples.name: Global complete intersection case for Cohen–Macaulay hypotheses
examples.note: In response to a question, global complete intersections are mentioned as a familiar situation where the older Cohen–Macaulay-type assumptions in the history of the theorem are satisfied.
examples.engagement_reason: global complete intersections are only named as a case ensuring the Cohen–Macaulay hypothesis, with no verification or computation

passes.reason: A global complete intersection is cut out by the minimal number of equations equal to its codimension and is used as a sufficient Cohen–Macaulay case.
A global complete intersection is an equality/minimal-equations case relative to codimension and is used only as a sufficient Cohen–Macaulay situation.
A global complete intersection is cited as a standard systematic situation ensuring the Cohen–Macaulay hypothesis, not as a failure case or classification exception.
global complete intersections form a standard systematic class used as a positive case ensuring Cohen–Macaulayness.
a global complete intersection is cut out by the minimal codimension number of equations and is used as a sufficient Cohen–Macaulay case, not adversarially.
The global complete-intersection case is a standard systematic sufficient hypothesis for Cohen–Macaulayness, not an adversarial or sporadic example.
A global complete intersection is the minimal-number-of-equations case ensuring Cohen–Macaulayness, cited as a positive sufficient case.
Global complete intersections are cited as a standard source of the Cohen–Macaulay hypothesis, not as an obstruction or exceptional case.
A global complete intersection is the minimal-equation/equality case ensuring Cohen–Macaulayness, cited as a sufficient case rather than a failure.
a global complete intersection is cut out by the minimal number of equations for its codimension and is used as a sufficient Cohen–Macaulay case
A global complete intersection is cited as a sufficient Cohen–Macaulay case and is cut out by the minimal number of defining equations.
The global complete intersection case is offered as a standard sufficient situation ensuring the Cohen–Macaulay hypothesis, not as a failure case or extreme object.

examples.name: High-weight motivic cohomology as étale cohomology
examples.note: For a smooth algebraically closed scheme of dimension at most the weight, the mod-prime-to-p motivic complex reduces to the usual étale cohomology complex, making the abstract motivic construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker plugs the high-weight smooth case into the motivic formula and explains that the truncation disappears, yielding étale cohomology

passes.reason: The high-weight range is a boundary range where the motivic complex simplifies to étale cohomology.
The comparison occurs in the high-weight boundary range where the truncation no longer changes the étale complex.
The high-weight range is a boundary regime where motivic cohomology reduces to étale cohomology, and it is used illustratively rather than adversarially.
the high-weight range is a boundary regime where the motivic complex simplifies to étale cohomology, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
the high-weight range is a boundary regime where the truncation becomes inactive and motivic cohomology identifies with étale cohomology.
The motivic complex is considered in the high-weight range relative to dimension, a boundary regime where it identifies with étale cohomology.
The weight is beyond the dimension so the truncation is in a high-range boundary case where motivic cohomology reduces to étale cohomology.
The high-weight range is used as a simplifying illustrative case where motivic cohomology agrees with étale cohomology, and it lies at an extreme large-weight/stable range.
The high-weight range is an extreme range where the truncation disappears and motivic cohomology identifies with étale cohomology.
the high-weight range is beyond the dimension bound where the truncation becomes inactive, giving étale cohomology rather than a refutation
The high-weight range is an endpoint regime where the truncation disappears and motivic cohomology becomes étale cohomology.
The high-weight range is used as a simplifying example where motivic cohomology identifies with étale cohomology, and it lies at an extreme weight-versus-dimension range.
